questions.passage:
In the last decades of the eighteenth century, English travelers began going to the mountains on purpose. The Lake District, previously a place one crossed with regret, became a destination, and an apparatus grew up to serve the new appetite. William Gilpin's illustrated tours taught readers which prospects deserved the name picturesque and which fell short. Guidebooks marked numbered viewing stations around the lakes, so that a visitor could proceed from one prescribed emotion to the next. Strangest of all was the Claude glass, a small tinted convex mirror named for a French landscape painter: the tourist turned his back on the actual scenery in order to admire its reflection, reduced, framed, and warmed to the amber tones of an old master. It is the perfect emblem of the enterprise, awe with its back to the mountain.

The satirists noticed at once, and their heirs are with us still. The charge has kept its shape for two centuries: such feeling is counterfeit. The genuine sublime, the argument runs, is an unbidden encounter between a solitary spirit and an overwhelming world, and an ecstasy scheduled at station number four, on the advice of a guidebook, in imitation of a painting, is a performance of emotion rather than the thing itself. Real awe cannot be an itinerary.

The suspicion is understandable, and anyone who has watched a crowd photograph a waterfall without looking at it will feel its force. But as a theory of feeling it rests on a premise that will not survive inspection: that there exists, somewhere behind culture, a natural eye, and that what this eye feels unprompted is authentic while what it feels on instruction is not. No such eye has ever gone on tour. The capacity to find mountains magnificent, rather than merely inconvenient and dangerous, was itself a cultural accomplishment, built by theology, painting, and poetry over generations; the shepherds who lived beneath those fells did not swoon at them. Every traveler who ever felt anything arrived already tutored, whether by Gilpin, by a landscape hanging in a parlor, or by descriptions absorbed so early they feel like instinct. The choice is never between scripted and unscripted perception. It is between scripts, and among ways of holding them.

Nor is tutored feeling necessarily faint or false. A listener who has studied a score often hears more in the symphony, not less; knowing where the modulation comes does not prevent the shiver, and frequently produces it. There is no reason the guidebook cannot function as the score does, sharpening attention, directing it to what repays it, and leaving the traveler more capable of being moved rather than less. Preparation is not the opposite of experience. It is often the condition of it.

And yet the satirists cannot be entirely dismissed, because scripts hold their readers in different ways. A prompt can open perception or close it. The tourist who consults the guidebook, looks up, and keeps looking has used the script as a door; the tourist who verifies that the view matches its description, collects the sensation named on the page, and turns away has used it as a receipt. The Claude glass could serve either master: an exercise in trained attention for one traveler, a portable machine for not looking for another. The instrument does not decide; the user does.

So the interesting question about the picturesque tourists, and about their descendants filing toward every famous prospect on earth, is not whether their feeling was authentic, a question that dissolves once the natural eye goes, but whether their scripts enlarged their attention or replaced it. Some were changed by what they saw. Some collected views like stamps. The mountain, indifferent to both, kept its counsel, as it keeps it now.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Eighteenth-century tourism destroyed the possibility of genuine encounters with landscape.
choices.B: The shepherds of the Lake District appreciated the fells more honestly than any visitor could.
choices.C: The popularity of the Claude glass proves that picturesque travelers were interested in admiring paintings rather than in encountering nature itself.
choices.D: Because all perception is culturally tutored, the real question about scripted encounters is whether the script enlarges attention or replaces it.

explanations.A: The passage holds that some tourists 'were changed by what they saw,' so scripted travel is compatible with genuine encounter; no destruction is claimed.
explanations.B: The shepherds appear to show the opposite point: living beneath the fells without tutoring, they 'did not swoon at them,' which undercuts the idea of an untaught, more honest appreciation.
explanations.C: The Claude glass is called an emblem that 'could serve either master,' trained attention or refusal to look; the passage explicitly says the instrument does not decide.
explanations.D: Correct. After dismantling the premise of a 'natural eye,' the passage concludes that the question of authenticity dissolves and is replaced by the question 'whether their scripts enlarged their attention or replaced it.'

questions.stem: The passage presents the Claude glass primarily as:

choices.A: an emblem of the mediated character of picturesque tourism, whose effect depended on how its user held the script.
choices.B: a genuine technical improvement that made remote and inaccessible scenery visible to ordinary travelers for the first time.
choices.C: a hoax perpetrated by guidebook publishers on credulous tourists.
choices.D: an instrument whose popularity proves that eighteenth-century travelers felt no awe at all.

explanations.A: Correct. The mirror is introduced as 'the perfect emblem of the enterprise, awe with its back to the mountain,' and returns near the end as a device that 'could serve either master,' trained attention or evasion, depending on the traveler.
explanations.B: The glass reduced and tinted scenery already before the viewer; nothing in the passage credits it with revealing anything previously invisible.
explanations.C: The mirror is described as a genuine and widely used instrument of taste; no deception by publishers is alleged.
explanations.D: The passage insists that tutored feeling can be real, and that some scripted travelers 'were changed by what they saw,' so the mirror's popularity licenses no conclusion that awe was absent.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the satirists who call tourist feeling counterfeit is best described as:

choices.A: complete and unreserved agreement with both their diagnosis of tourism and their underlying theory of feeling.
choices.B: contemptuous rejection of an objection he finds entirely without merit.
choices.C: puzzlement about what their criticism is meant to accomplish.
choices.D: sympathy for their observation combined with rejection of the theory of feeling beneath it.

explanations.A: The author dismantles the satirists' central premise, the 'natural eye,' at length, so full agreement is ruled out.
explanations.B: He concedes their observation real force, invoking the crowd photographing a waterfall without looking, and grants near the end that 'the satirists cannot be entirely dismissed.'
explanations.C: The author understands the criticism perfectly well; he restates it carefully before answering it, showing engagement rather than puzzlement.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage calls the suspicion 'understandable' and preserves its kernel in the door-versus-receipt distinction, while rejecting the premise 'that there exists, somewhere behind culture, a natural eye' on which the charge of counterfeit feeling depends.

questions.stem: Which of the following best exemplifies the passage's claim that preparation can be the condition of experience rather than its opposite?

choices.A: A concertgoer who has studied the symphony's score and finds that knowing where the modulation arrives intensifies her response to it.
choices.B: A hiker who refuses to read anything about a mountain range before visiting it.
choices.C: A tourist who photographs a famous prospect and reviews the image only after returning home.
choices.D: A reader who carefully memorizes a guidebook's descriptions in advance in order to recite them word for word at each numbered viewing station.

explanations.A: Correct. This is the passage's own analogy pressed into a case: the tutored listener 'often hears more in the symphony, not less,' and foreknowledge of the modulation 'does not prevent the shiver, and frequently produces it,' preparation functioning as the condition of being moved.
explanations.B: Deliberate ignorance pursues the myth of the untutored encounter, the very 'natural eye' the passage argues has never existed.
explanations.C: Deferring all attention to a later photograph is the passage's image of not looking, closer to the receipt than to any prepared experience.
explanations.D: Recitation at each station treats the script as something to be matched and performed, the collecting of named sensations the passage identifies as the script replacing attention.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings, if true, would most weaken the author's argument that guided viewing can sharpen rather than replace attention?

choices.A: Sales of the Claude glass declined sharply after the 1820s.
choices.B: Some eighteenth-century travelers visited the Lake District without purchasing any guidebook.
choices.C: Systematic comparisons show that travelers using viewing-station guides looked more briefly, echoed the guides' phrases, and reported weaker responses than unguided travelers.
choices.D: Gilpin privately admitted in his correspondence that he had knowingly exaggerated the picturesque qualities of several celebrated views.

explanations.A: The instrument's later commercial fortunes say nothing about what guided attention did to those who used it in its heyday.
explanations.B: The existence of unguided travelers is fully compatible with the claim that guidance can sharpen attention for those who use it.
explanations.C: Correct. The author's position is that a script can 'function as the score does, sharpening attention' and leaving travelers 'more capable of being moved'; systematic evidence that guided viewers looked less, borrowed the script's phrases, and felt less would indicate that in practice the guides operated as receipts rather than doors.
explanations.D: Gilpin's exaggerations would impugn one guide's honesty, not the general claim about what prepared attention can do for perception.

questions.stem: It can most reasonably be inferred that the author believes a traveler who arrives at a famous prospect without any guidebook:

choices.A: will experience the mountain scenery exactly as the local shepherds who lived beneath it once did.
choices.B: still perceives the scene through cultural tutoring absorbed elsewhere.
choices.C: is more likely than a guided traveler to be genuinely moved.
choices.D: has escaped the influence of painting and poetry on landscape taste.

explanations.A: The shepherds mark the absence of the relevant tutoring, and the passage implies a modern traveler cannot recover that condition, having absorbed the taste for mountains 'so early it feels like instinct.'
explanations.B: Correct. The passage states that 'every traveler who ever felt anything arrived already tutored,' whether by Gilpin, a parlor landscape, or early descriptions, so leaving the guidebook behind removes one script without removing scriptedness.
explanations.C: The passage declines to rank guided and unguided travelers this way; what matters in its account is how any script is held, as door or receipt, not whether a printed one is carried.
explanations.D: Painting and poetry are named among the forces that built the very capacity to find mountains magnificent, an inheritance no traveler sheds by traveling light.
